claimed_contribution: Introduces brand engagement in self-concept (BESC) as a generalized individual difference capturing consumers' propensity to include important brands in how they view themselves.
Develops and validates an eight-item BESC scale, with evidence of reliability and nomological/discriminant validity versus related constructs (including material values and social self constructs).
Shows that BESC has practical explanatory value by predicting brand-related memory, attention, product evaluation, and loyalty-relevant intentions.
context: The paper studies consumer-brand relationships in general consumer goods settings, using favorite brands as the focal reference. Empirical contexts include student possessions, incidental exposure to branded clothing in photos, and hypothetical new-product decisions in portable DVD players.
design_and_data: Scale development followed standard psychometric procedures: 36 initial items, expert content evaluation, and purification on an undergraduate sample (N = 430) to an 8-item scale (reported alpha = .94, with test-retest reliability reported in a range). Nomological validity was examined with four independent undergraduate samples using correlations with related constructs (e.g., RISC, material values, self-esteem, self-concept clarity, social desirability). Validation then used five studies: an idiographic IAT study with favorite vs least favorite brands (N = 116, usable N = 106), a branded-possession recall study among campus residents (N = 56), an incidental brand-exposure photo study (N = 42), a logo presence/absence experiment for Nike/Ralph Lauren products (N = 153), and two new-product scenario experiments on price sensitivity (N = 62) and waiting-time sensitivity (N = 126).
main_findings: Higher BESC was associated with stronger self-favorite-brand memory links in an IAT and with greater recall of owned and incidentally seen brand names.

When brand attitudes were favorable, high-BESC consumers evaluated logo-present products more positively than logo-absent versions, whereas low-BESC consumers showed little change with logo visibility.

Higher BESC was associated with greater loyalty-related insensitivity: compared with low-BESC consumers, high-BESC consumers were less affected by higher prices and were more willing to wait for a favorite brand's new product.
one_line_summary: The paper develops an eight-item BESC scale and shows across multiple studies that consumers higher in BESC have stronger self-brand links and display distinct market responses, including greater brand attention/recall, stronger preference for overtly branded favorites, and lower price and time sensitivity for favorite brands.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior self-brand connection and attachment-to-possession research by moving from brand-specific links to a generalized person-level propensity to integrate important brands into the self-concept, then testing its downstream behavioral relevance.
research_gap: Prior work mainly measured self-connections to specific brands or possessions rather than a general tendency to engage brands in the self-concept across multiple brands. The paper argues that marketing research lacked a validated general measure of this tendency and evidence that it predicts key brand-related attitudes and behaviors.
research_question: Do consumers differ in a generalized propensity to include important brands in their self-concept, and can this propensity be measured reliably as BESC? If so, does BESC predict consequential brand-related outcomes such as memory, attention, product evaluations, and loyalty-related intentions?
